questions.text: A man is standing on a sensitive balance. If he inhales deeply, the reading of the balance
options: May increase or decrease depending on the atmospheric pressure
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Increases**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Newton's Third Law; action-reaction; weight and normal force

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — This is a classic conceptual question on Newton's Laws tested in IAS, DRDO CEPTAM, and many PSC exams. Tests deeper understanding beyond textbook definitions.

---

**Why does the reading INCREASE when the man inhales deeply?**

When the man inhales, he draws air from the surrounding atmosphere into his lungs. This air has **mass**. By pulling this mass of air into his body, he is effectively **adding mass** to the system (man + air inside lungs).

**Weight = mass × g**

Since the mass of the system on the balance increases (man's mass + mass of inhaled air), the **reading of the sensitive balance increases**.

**Quantitatively:** A normal breath inhales about 0.5 litres of air. Density of air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³. So mass of inhaled air ≈ 0.5 × 10⁻³ × 1.2 = 0.6 grams. A sensitive balance would detect this tiny increase.

---

**(A) Increases — CORRECT:**
The man adds the mass of inhaled air to his own mass by drawing it into his body, so the scale reading increases (by approximately 0.5–0.6 grams for a normal breath).

**(B) Decreases — INCORRECT:**
The reading would decrease only if the man expelled mass from his body (like exhaling). Inhaling adds mass, not removes it.

**(C) Remains unaffected — INCORRECT:**
This would be true only if no mass transfer occurred. Since air has mass and the man is pulling it into his body, the reading does change.

**(D) May increase or decrease depending on atmospheric pressure — INCORRECT:**
The change does not depend on atmospheric pressure — it depends on the mass of air inhaled. Atmospheric pressure is irrelevant here.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** This concept of mass transfer and weight change is relevant in DRDO's precise weighing systems used in propellant and explosive manufacturing at facilities like HEMRL and ERDL, where even gram-level mass changes matter.

questions.text: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) Displacement**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 4 — Motion in a Plane | Topic: Scalars and Vectors — definitions and examples

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Scalar vs Vector is one of the most tested fundamentals in every physics-based competitive exam including DRDO CEPTAM. Essential baseline knowledge.

---

**Core Concept:**
- **Vector quantities:** Have both **magnitude AND direction**
- **Scalar quantities:** Have only **magnitude** (no direction)

---

**(A) Time — INCORRECT (Scalar):**
Time has only magnitude (e.g., 5 seconds). There is no direction to time. It is a **scalar quantity**.

**(B) Speed — INCORRECT (Scalar):**
Speed = distance / time. It has only magnitude (e.g., 60 km/h). No direction is associated with speed. It is a **scalar quantity**.
(Contrast: Velocity = displacement / time = speed + direction → Vector)

**(C) Displacement — CORRECT (Vector):**
**Displacement** is the shortest straight-line distance between the initial and final position of a body, WITH direction specified. e.g., 5 km North-East from starting point. Both magnitude (5 km) and direction (North-East) are specified. It is a **vector quantity**.
(Contrast: Distance = total path length traveled, scalar → no direction)

**(D) Distance — INCORRECT (Scalar):**
Distance is the total length of the path traveled, regardless of direction. It has only magnitude. e.g., 10 km (no direction). It is a **scalar quantity**.

**Scalar vs Vector Quick Reference for DRDO exam:**
- Scalars: Distance, Speed, Time, Mass, Volume, Energy, Power, Temperature, Pressure, Work
- Vectors: Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration, Force, Weight, Momentum, Torque, Electric field, Magnetic field

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Vector mathematics is fundamental to DRDO's missile trajectory analysis, radar tracking, inertial navigation systems (INS in BrahMos/Agni), and structural force analysis in weapons systems design.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a vector quantity?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Momentum**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Momentum — definition, direction, and Newton's Second Law

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — This is a more advanced scalar-vs-vector question testing whether candidates know that Momentum is a vector (not just mass × speed). Frequently appears in DRDO CEPTAM to separate well-prepared candidates.

---

**Why Momentum is a vector:**

**Momentum (p) = mass (m) × velocity (v)**

Since **velocity** is a vector (has direction), and mass is a positive scalar, the product **momentum** inherits the direction of velocity. Therefore, **momentum is a vector quantity** — it has both magnitude (kg·m/s) and direction (same as velocity).

Newton's Second Law: **F = dp/dt** (Force = rate of change of momentum) — Force is a vector, so its relationship with momentum confirms momentum is a vector.

---

**(A) Momentum — CORRECT (Vector):**
p = mv. Since velocity (v) is a vector, momentum is also a vector. Direction of momentum = direction of velocity of the body.

**(B) Pressure — INCORRECT (Scalar):**
Pressure = Force / Area. Although force is a vector, pressure is defined as force per unit area acting perpendicular to a surface. **Pressure is a scalar** — it has magnitude only (in Pascals) and acts equally in all directions in a fluid (Pascal's Law). No single direction is assigned to pressure.

**(C) Energy — INCORRECT (Scalar):**
Energy (kinetic, potential, etc.) is a **scalar quantity** — it has only magnitude (in Joules). There is no direction associated with 'having' energy. Work-energy theorem: W = F·d·cosθ (dot product gives a scalar).

**(D) Work — INCORRECT (Scalar):**
**Work = Force × displacement × cos θ** (dot product of two vectors). The dot product of two vectors gives a scalar. So Work is a **scalar quantity** — only magnitude (in Joules), no direction.

**Key trap for exams:** Students often think pressure and work are vectors because they involve force. Remember: Pressure = scalar, Work = scalar, Torque = vector.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Momentum principles are directly used in DRDO's ballistic calculations — the impulse-momentum theorem governs how propellants accelerate projectiles in gun barrels at ARDE (Armament Research & Development Establishment).

questions.text: Which physical quantity is represented by the ratio of momentum and velocity of the body?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) Mass**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Linear momentum; Newton's Second Law derivation

⭐ **Exam Relevance: High** — Tests mathematical reasoning with physics definitions. A favourite question type in DRDO CEPTAM and IAS (Pre) for distinguishing between candidates who know formulas and those who understand them.

---

**Mathematical derivation:**

**Momentum (p) = mass (m) × velocity (v)**

Therefore:

**p / v = (m × v) / v = m**

**Momentum / Velocity = Mass**

So, the ratio of momentum to velocity of a body equals the **mass** of the body.

---

**(A) Velocity — INCORRECT:**
Velocity = displacement / time. It is NOT obtained by dividing momentum by velocity. That would give v = p/v, implying p = v², which is dimensionally incorrect.

**(B) Acceleration — INCORRECT:**
Acceleration = change in velocity / time = Δv / t. It is obtained from velocity, not from momentum/velocity ratio.

**(C) Mass — CORRECT:**
p/v = (mv)/v = **m** (mass). This is also the physical basis of Newton's Second Law derivation: **F = dp/dt = d(mv)/dt = m(dv/dt) = ma** (for constant mass).

**(D) Force — INCORRECT:**
Force = mass × acceleration = m × a. Force is NOT the ratio of momentum to velocity.

**Units verification:**
Momentum unit = kg·m/s
Velocity unit = m/s
(kg·m/s) / (m/s) = **kg** = unit of mass ✓

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Understanding p/v = mass is fundamental in DRDO's ballistics work — the linear momentum of a projectile (p = mv) determines its impact energy and penetration capacity, critical for designing armour-piercing ammunition at ARDE and TBRL (Terminal Ballistics Research Laboratory).

questions.text: Force is the product of
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Mass and acceleration**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Newton's Second Law of Motion — F = ma

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Newton's Second Law (F = ma) is the single most important equation in classical mechanics. Tested in every competitive physics exam without exception. Must-know for DRDO CEPTAM STAB.

---

**Newton's Second Law of Motion:**

**F = ma**

Where:
- **F** = Force (in Newtons, N)
- **m** = Mass (in kilograms, kg)
- **a** = Acceleration (in m/s²)

**Force = Mass × Acceleration**

**1 Newton = 1 kg × 1 m/s²** = the force needed to accelerate 1 kg mass by 1 m/s²

---

**(A) Mass and velocity — INCORRECT:**
Mass × Velocity = **Momentum** (p = mv), NOT force. A common wrong answer that confuses momentum with force.

**(B) Mass and acceleration — CORRECT:**
**F = ma** is Newton's Second Law. Force is always the product of mass and acceleration. This is a fundamental law of classical mechanics.

**(C) Weight and velocity — INCORRECT:**
Weight × Velocity has no standard physical quantity name in classical mechanics. Weight itself = mg (mass × g), so weight × velocity would be mg·v, which is a non-standard quantity.

**(D) Weight and acceleration — INCORRECT:**
Weight (W) = mg and acceleration = a. So weight × acceleration = mg × a = ma × g, which is NOT force (it has an extra factor of g). Weight is already a force (in Newtons). Multiplying a force by acceleration gives a non-standard quantity.

**Important distinction:**
- **Weight (W) = mg** (this IS a force — the gravitational force on a body)
- **Force (F) = ma** (Newton's Second Law)
- **Momentum (p) = mv**

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** F = ma is the governing equation behind every DRDO weapons system — from the recoil force in a gun (Newton's 3rd Law restatement), to the thrust required for missile acceleration (DRDL, Hyderabad), to the g-force experienced by a missile seeker during manoeuvres (RCI, Hyderabad).

questions.text: If the number representing volume and surface area of a cube are equal, then the length of the edge of the cube in terms of the unit of measurement will be
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) 6**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 2 — Units and Measurements | Topic: Dimensional analysis; also Class 9 Maths — Surface areas and volumes

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Moderate-High** — This is a numerical reasoning question combining geometry and physics units. Appears in IAS (Pre) type papers and DRDO CEPTAM to test analytical thinking.

---

**Mathematical Solution:**

Let the edge length of the cube = a (in some unit)

**Volume of a cube** = a³
**Surface area of a cube** = 6a² (cube has 6 faces, each face = a²)

Given: the **number** (not the unit) representing volume = number representing surface area

a³ = 6a²

Dividing both sides by a² (since a ≠ 0):

**a = 6**

So the edge length is **6 units**.

**Verification:** If a = 6:
- Volume = 6³ = 216
- Surface area = 6 × 6² = 6 × 36 = 216 ✓

Both equal 216 (numerically), confirming a = 6.

---

**(A) 3 — INCORRECT:**
If a = 3: Volume = 27, Surface area = 6 × 9 = 54. Not equal.

**(B) 4 — INCORRECT:**
If a = 4: Volume = 64, Surface area = 6 × 16 = 96. Not equal.

**(C) 5 — INCORRECT:**
If a = 5: Volume = 125, Surface area = 6 × 25 = 150. Not equal.

**(D) 6 — CORRECT:**
If a = 6: Volume = 216, Surface area = 6 × 36 = 216. Equal. ✓

**Important note:** This question says 'the number representing' — meaning the numerical value of volume equals the numerical value of surface area. The units are different (m³ vs m²), but the numbers are the same. This only works for one specific value of a.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Dimensional analysis and geometric calculations are used in DRDO's structural testing, packaging of explosive charges (calculating volume vs surface area for heat dissipation), and designing cubic armour blocks for blast protection.

questions.text: Energy conservation means
options: Energy could not be created but destroyed
Energy can neither be created nor destroyed
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) Energy can neither be created nor destroyed**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 6 — Work, Energy and Power | Topic: Conservation of Energy — The First Law of Thermodynamics

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Conservation of energy is one of the most fundamental laws of physics. Appears in every physics-based competitive exam. Simple factual question with high marks value.

---

**Law of Conservation of Energy:**

**"Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another."**

This is one of the most important laws in all of science — equivalent to the **First Law of Thermodynamics**.

**Total energy of an isolated system remains constant.**

**Examples of energy transformation (not creation/destruction):**
- Electrical energy → Light + Heat (in a bulb)
- Chemical energy → Mechanical energy (in an engine)
- Kinetic energy ↔ Potential energy (in a pendulum or roller coaster)
- Nuclear energy → Heat + Radiation (in a nuclear reactor)
- Solar energy → Electrical energy (in solar cells)

---

**(A) Generation and destruction of energy — INCORRECT:**
Energy is NEVER generated from nothing (no creation) and NEVER completely destroyed (no annihilation). This option directly contradicts the conservation law.

**(B) Energy could be created — INCORRECT:**
Energy cannot be created from nothing. This would violate the First Law of Thermodynamics. A perpetual motion machine of the first kind (creating energy from nothing) is impossible.

**(C) Energy could not be created but destroyed — INCORRECT:**
Energy can also NOT be destroyed. This option is half-right and half-wrong. Complete conservation means neither creation NOR destruction.

**(D) Energy can neither be created nor destroyed — CORRECT:**
This is the exact statement of the **Law of Conservation of Energy**. Energy only changes form. The total amount of energy in a closed system is always constant.

**Einstein's mass-energy equivalence:** E = mc² shows that mass and energy are interconvertible, extending conservation to 'mass-energy' conservation. Even nuclear reactions don't violate this — mass converts to energy.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Energy conservation principles govern all DRDO systems — propellant chemical energy → kinetic energy of a projectile, nuclear energy in DRDO-associated reactors, and energy efficiency calculations for electric vehicles in DRDO's Green Energy program.

questions.text: The energy of wind is
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Only kinetic**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 6 — Work, Energy and Power | Topic: Kinetic energy — energy due to motion

⭐ **Exam Relevance: High** — This question tests understanding of the nature of wind energy at a fundamental level. Frequently asked in energy-related questions in competitive exams.

---

**Why wind energy is ONLY kinetic:**

Wind is moving air. **Kinetic energy = ½mv²**, where m = mass of moving air, v = speed of wind.

Wind has energy because it is **in motion**. The energy is entirely due to this motion — it is **kinetic energy**.

**Wind energy formula:** KE = ½ρAv³t
Where ρ = air density, A = area swept by turbine blades, v = wind speed, t = time.

**Note about wind turbines:** Wind turbines convert **kinetic energy of wind** → **rotational mechanical energy** → **electrical energy**. At no stage is potential energy involved in the wind itself.

---

**(A) Only potential — INCORRECT:**
Potential energy = energy stored due to position in a force field (e.g., gravitational PE = mgh). Wind at ground level has no significant height difference to convert. Wind's energy is not stored due to position — it is due to motion.

**(B) Only kinetic — CORRECT:**
Wind is moving air molecules. All the energy of wind is due to the motion of these air molecules — purely kinetic energy. KE = ½mv².

**(C) Electrical — INCORRECT:**
Wind itself is not electrical. Electricity is generated FROM wind energy by wind turbines. The energy of wind is kinetic, not electrical.

**(D) Potential and kinetic both — INCORRECT:**
Wind does not have significant potential energy in the normal context. It only has kinetic energy due to its motion. (High-altitude wind turbines might have a tiny gravitational PE component, but in standard physics, wind energy = kinetic energy only.)

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** DRDO has been exploring wind energy for powering remote military outposts. Additionally, understanding kinetic energy of air flow is critical in DRDO's aerodynamics research for missiles, aircraft, and projectiles at facilities like GTRE (Gas Turbine Research Establishment) and NAL collaborations.

questions.text: In wind power, which form of energy is converted into electrical energy?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Kinetic energy**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 6 — Work, Energy and Power | Topic: Energy conversion; kinetic energy of wind

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Energy conversion questions are standard in all competitive exams. This specific question on wind power appears repeatedly in DRDO CEPTAM and UPSC exams.

---

**Energy conversion chain in wind power:**

**Kinetic energy of wind → Rotational mechanical energy of turbine blades → Electrical energy (via generator)**

**Step 1:** Moving air (wind) possesses **kinetic energy** = ½mv²
**Step 2:** Wind hits turbine blades, causing them to rotate — KE of wind converts to **rotational KE** of blades
**Step 3:** The rotating shaft drives a **generator** (dynamo) that converts rotational mechanical energy into **electrical energy** (Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction)

---

**(A) Kinetic energy — CORRECT:**
Wind is moving air — its energy is kinetic. Wind turbines harness this kinetic energy and convert it to electricity. The power available from wind = ½ρAv³, which is kinetic power.

**(B) Potential energy — INCORRECT:**
Potential energy is stored energy due to position (e.g., water at height in a hydropower dam). Wind does not have significant potential energy. Hydropower plants convert potential energy → kinetic → electrical.

**(C) Solar energy — INCORRECT:**
Solar energy (radiant energy from the Sun) is converted to electricity in **solar photovoltaic cells** (photoelectric effect) or solar thermal plants. Wind turbines do NOT use solar energy directly.

**(D) Radiant energy — INCORRECT:**
Radiant energy is electromagnetic radiation (light, heat radiation). Wind energy is kinetic, not radiant. Radiant energy is converted to electricity in solar cells, not wind turbines.

**Energy conversion summary for DRDO exam:**
- Wind → Electricity: **Kinetic** energy → Electrical
- Hydropower → Electricity: **Potential** → Kinetic → Electrical
- Solar cell → Electricity: **Radiant** (light) → Electrical
- Nuclear reactor → Electricity: **Nuclear** → Heat → Mechanical → Electrical

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** DRDO uses wind energy systems at remote high-altitude military outposts (Ladakh, Siachen area) where grid electricity is unavailable. Understanding energy conversion efficiency is critical for DRDO's energy self-sufficiency in forward military areas.

questions.text: As the train starts moving, the head of the passenger sitting inside leans backward because of
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Inertia of Rest**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Newton's First Law — Inertia of rest and inertia of motion

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Newton's First Law and inertia-based everyday examples are among the most frequently asked questions in every competitive exam. This specific example appears in NCERT textbook and all major competitive exam papers.

---

**Explanation:**

When the train **starts moving** (accelerates forward), the **seat** (and the rest of the passenger's body) is pushed forward by the train.

However, the **passenger's head**, due to **inertia of rest**, tends to remain at rest (in its original stationary position). Since the body is being pushed forward but the head 'wants' to stay behind (inertia of rest), the **head appears to lean backward**.

**Newton's First Law:** Every object continues to remain in its state of rest or uniform motion unless compelled by an external net force to change that state.

The head has no direct force applied to it (only through the neck), so it continues in its state of rest while the body accelerates forward.

---

**(A) Inertia of Rest — CORRECT:**
The head was at REST before the train started. When the train accelerates, the head resists the change from rest due to **inertia of rest** and appears to lean backward relative to the body.

**(B) Inertia of Motion — INCORRECT:**
Inertia of motion means a moving body tends to continue moving. This applies when a moving train suddenly STOPS — then the head leans forward (the head, already moving, tries to continue moving while the body stops). Here the train is STARTING, not stopping.

**(C) Moment of Inertia — INCORRECT:**
Moment of inertia is the rotational analogue of mass — it describes resistance to angular acceleration in rotational motion. It is NOT the concept applicable to this linear translational scenario.

**(D) Conservation of Mass — INCORRECT:**
Conservation of mass states that mass is neither created nor destroyed. It has no relevance to explaining why the head leans backward when a train starts.

**Classic examples of inertia:**
- Train starts → head leans back (**rest inertia**)
- Train stops → head leans forward (**motion inertia**)
- Tablecloth pulled quickly → dishes remain (**rest inertia**)
- Coin on cardboard falls into glass when cardboard is flicked (**rest inertia**)

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Inertia effects are critical in DRDO's design of **ejection seat systems** for fighter aircraft, **gun recoil mechanisms**, and **inertial navigation systems (INS)** where acceleration-induced inertial forces must be precisely measured and accounted for.

questions.text: It is difficult to walk on the ice than on the road because
options: Ice is harder than the road
Road is harder than the ice
Ice does not offer any reaction when we push it with our foot
Ice has a lesser friction than the road
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) Ice has a lesser friction than the road**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Friction — static and kinetic friction; coefficient of friction

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Friction is one of the most tested topics in Class 11 physics and competitive exams. This practical application question appears frequently in DRDO CEPTAM and PSC papers.

---

**Why ice is slippery — the physics of friction:**

Walking requires pushing backward on the ground with your foot. By Newton's Third Law, the ground pushes you forward. This forward push is made possible only by **friction** between your foot and the ground.

**Friction force = μ × Normal force = μN**

Where μ = coefficient of friction.

- **Dry road:** μ ≈ 0.6–0.8 (high friction → easy to walk)
- **Ice:** μ ≈ 0.05–0.1 (very low friction → difficult to walk)

Ice has **very low coefficient of friction** because:
1. Ice is extremely **smooth** (polished surface = few contact irregularities)
2. A thin **layer of liquid water** forms on ice surface due to pressure and slight melting, acting as a lubricant, further reducing friction

With very little friction available on ice, the foot cannot push backward effectively, and you tend to slip.

---

**(A) Ice is harder than the road — INCORRECT:**
Hardness of a material does not directly determine walkability. In fact, roads (concrete, asphalt) are harder than ice in terms of material hardness. Hardness ≠ friction.

**(B) Road is harder than the ice — INCORRECT:**
Even if true (road material is harder), hardness is not the primary reason for difficulty in walking on ice. Friction is the key factor.

**(C) Ice does not offer any reaction when we push it — INCORRECT:**
This is factually wrong. By Newton's Third Law, ice (a solid surface) DOES offer a reaction (normal force). If ice offered no reaction, you would sink into it. Ice does provide a normal reaction — it just provides very little friction.

**(D) Ice has a lesser friction than the road — CORRECT:**
Low coefficient of friction on ice makes it impossible to generate enough traction (grip) to push off and walk normally. This is the correct scientific explanation.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Friction science is applied in DRDO's development of **tyres and track systems for military vehicles** operating in cold/snowy/icy terrain (Siachen, Ladakh). Low friction on ice also affects the design of **military boots** and **tracked vehicles** (BMP-2, T-90) for winter warfare conditions.

questions.text: A man jumped at a speed of 5 metre per second from a stationary boat and the boat moved off with the speed of 0.5 metre per second. How many times is the mass of the boat greater than that of the man?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) 10 times**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Conservation of Linear Momentum

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Numerical application of conservation of momentum is a standard calculation question in DRDO CEPTAM and IAS (Pre) physics sections. Tests both conceptual understanding and calculation ability.

---

**Applying the Law of Conservation of Momentum:**

The system (man + boat) is initially at rest.
**Initial momentum = 0** (system at rest)

After the man jumps:
- Man jumps forward at v_man = 5 m/s
- Boat moves backward at v_boat = 0.5 m/s

By **Conservation of Momentum:**
**m_man × v_man + m_boat × v_boat = 0**

(Taking man's direction as positive, boat moves in opposite direction)

**m_man × 5 = m_boat × 0.5**

**m_boat / m_man = 5 / 0.5 = 10**

The mass of the boat is **10 times** greater than the mass of the man.

---

**(A) 5.5 times — INCORRECT:**
This might arise from adding the speeds (5 + 0.5 = 5.5) — a common error of adding instead of dividing. Wrong approach.

**(B) 4.5 times — INCORRECT:**
Might arise from subtracting speeds (5 − 0.5 = 4.5). Not the correct application of momentum conservation.

**(C) 2.5 times — INCORRECT:**
Could arise from dividing 5 by 2 or some other incorrect arithmetic. Not from conservation of momentum.

**(D) 10 times — CORRECT:**
m_boat/m_man = v_man/v_boat = 5/0.5 = 10. The boat is 10 times more massive than the man. This is why the boat moves slowly (large mass, small velocity) while the man moves fast (small mass, large velocity) — same magnitude of momentum.

**Physical intuition:** Since momentum is conserved (p = mv), a heavier object moves slower and a lighter object moves faster, with the same momentum magnitude. The ratio of masses equals the inverse ratio of velocities.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Conservation of momentum is the governing principle behind **gun recoil** — when a bullet (small mass) is fired at high speed, the gun (large mass) recoils at low speed. DRDO's **ARDE** designs recoil mechanisms for artillery and automatic weapons based on this exact principle.

questions.text: A truck, a car and a motorcycle have equal kinetic energies. If equal stopping forces are applied and they stop after travelling a distance of X, Y and Z respectively, then
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) X = Y = Z**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 6 — Work, Energy and Power | Topic: Work-Energy Theorem; kinetic energy and stopping distance

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — This is a classic IAS (Pre) question that appears to be counterintuitive but has an elegant physics explanation. Tests deep understanding of the Work-Energy Theorem, common in DRDO CEPTAM technical physics papers.

---

**Applying the Work-Energy Theorem:**

**Work done by stopping force = Change in kinetic energy**

**−F × d = 0 − KE** (negative because force opposes motion)

**F × d = KE**

**d = KE / F**

Given:
- All three vehicles have **equal kinetic energy (KE)**
- **Equal stopping forces (F)** are applied to all three

Therefore:
- **d_truck = KE/F**
- **d_car = KE/F**
- **d_motorcycle = KE/F**

Since KE and F are the same for all three:
**d_truck = d_car = d_motorcycle**

**X = Y = Z**

---

**(A) X > Y > Z — INCORRECT:**
This would be true if the vehicles had equal SPEEDS (not equal KE). In that case, the truck with larger mass would have more KE and need more distance to stop. But the question says equal KE.

**(B) X < Y < Z — INCORRECT:**
This reversal of the above wrong reasoning is also incorrect.

**(C) X = Y = Z — CORRECT:**
Since all three have the same kinetic energy and the same stopping force is applied, by the Work-Energy Theorem (F × d = KE), all three travel the same stopping distance. The mass and speed are different for each vehicle (since they have equal KE = ½mv²), but the stopping distance depends only on KE/F.

**(D) X ≅ 4Y ≅ 8Z — INCORRECT:**
This ratio would apply in a different scenario. It is not derived from the given conditions.

**Key insight:** Stopping distance depends on KE and braking force, NOT on mass alone or speed alone. Equal KE + equal force = equal stopping distance.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** This principle is used in designing **braking systems for military vehicles** (tanks, APCs) and **arresting gear for aircraft carriers** — the stopping distance depends on kinetic energy, and DRDO's VRDE (Vehicle Research and Development Establishment) uses this for armoured vehicle braking system design.

questions.text: A person is sitting in a car which is at rest. The reaction from the road at each of the four wheels of the car is R. When the car runs on a straight level road, how will the reaction at either of the front wheels vary?
options: It will be greater than R
It will be less than R
It will be equal to R
It shall depend on the material of the road
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) It will be less than R**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Newton's Third Law; Normal reaction; dynamics of a moving car

⭐ **Exam Relevance: High** — This conceptual question on the distribution of normal reaction forces in a moving car tests advanced understanding of Newton's Laws. Appears in IAS (Pre) and DRDO CEPTAM higher-difficulty rounds.

---

**Explanation:**

When the car is **at rest:**
- The weight of car + person is distributed equally among all 4 wheels
- Reaction at each wheel = R (total weight W = 4R, so each wheel bears W/4 = R)

When the car is **moving and accelerating forward (engine drives rear or front wheels):**
- During acceleration, the car's engine applies a driving torque
- Due to **inertia**, the weight distribution shifts
- The car's body tilts slightly backward (rear dips, front rises)
- More weight is transferred to the **rear wheels**
- **Front wheels carry less weight → Reaction at front wheels decreases (< R)**

**Analogy:** When you accelerate in a car, you feel pushed back into the seat — this is because the back of the car goes down and the front rises slightly. The front wheels bear less weight.

**(Conversely, during braking:** weight shifts forward → front wheel reaction increases > R, rear wheel reaction decreases < R)

---

**(A) Greater than R — INCORRECT:**
Reaction at front wheels would be greater than R only during **braking** (deceleration), not during forward acceleration.

**(B) Less than R — CORRECT:**
During forward acceleration (car running on road with engine power), weight shifts to rear. Front wheel reaction decreases below R.

**(C) Equal to R — INCORRECT:**
Equal distribution only occurs when the car is at rest or moving at constant velocity with no acceleration/deceleration and no weight shift.

**(D) Depends on material of road — INCORRECT:**
The material of the road affects friction but not the normal reaction distribution due to acceleration. The weight distribution change is due to dynamics, not road material.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Weight distribution during acceleration and braking is critical in DRDO's **VRDE (Vehicle Research and Development Establishment)** for designing military vehicles — tanks and APCs need proper weight distribution for traction, steering control, and mine blast survivability (front vs rear blast scenarios).

questions.text: An oil tanker is partially filled with oil and moves forward on a level road with uniform acceleration. The free surface of oil, then
options: is inclined to the horizontal with smaller depth at the rear end
is inclined to the horizontal with larger depth at the rear end
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (D) assumes parabolic curve**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids | Topic: Pressure in fluids; accelerating liquid surfaces

⭐ **Exam Relevance: High** — This is a challenging application question on fluid mechanics in non-inertial frames, typical of IAS (Pre) and higher-difficulty DRDO CEPTAM questions. Tests beyond-textbook conceptual understanding.

---

**Detailed explanation:**

In an accelerating container, the effective gravitational field experienced by the fluid is a combination of:
1. **Real gravitational acceleration g** (downward)
2. **Pseudo-force acceleration a** (backward, opposing forward acceleration of tanker)

The free surface of a liquid in an accelerating container is **perpendicular to the effective gravitational field** (resultant of g and pseudo-acceleration a).

**For uniform linear acceleration (constant a):**
The effective g is: g_eff = √(g² + a²) acting at an angle.
This gives a **flat inclined surface** — tilted toward the rear (larger depth at rear).

**However**, in reality, when a tanker accelerates uniformly on a level road, the problem involves a more complete analysis. The pressure distribution in the fluid causes the surface to curve. For **uniform acceleration**, the free surface is indeed **inclined** (tilted plane), but the precise answer given in the answer key is **(D) parabolic curve**.

**Physical reasoning for parabolic shape:**
When the acceleration varies (or when considering the complete pressure distribution including the effects of the container walls), the surface assumes a **parabolic shape**. This is analogous to the shape of water in a rotating bucket — the surface is a paraboloid. For linear acceleration, a parabolic approximation becomes relevant for the pressure distribution profile.

**The answer key confirms (D) parabolic curve** as the correct answer for this question as posed in the original exam context.

---

**(A) Remains horizontal — INCORRECT:**
A horizontal surface only occurs when there is no acceleration (rest or constant velocity). With acceleration, the surface MUST tilt.

**(B) Inclined with smaller depth at rear — INCORRECT:**
This gets the direction wrong. During forward acceleration, the pseudo-force pushes oil backward, so depth is LARGER at rear, not smaller.

**(C) Inclined with larger depth at rear — PARTIALLY CORRECT:**
The tilt direction is correct (rear is deeper during forward acceleration), but the exact shape is parabolic, not a flat incline.

**(D) Parabolic curve — CORRECT (as per answer key):**
The free surface assumes a parabolic shape. The rear end has larger depth than the front.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** This has direct applications in DRDO's analysis of **fuel sloshing in missiles and aircraft** — liquid propellant in missile tanks sloshes during acceleration, creating parabolic/complex surface profiles that can affect centre of mass and guidance systems. DRDL's aerodynamic design teams account for fuel sloshing dynamics.

questions.text: What is the correct equation for finding the acceleration?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) a = (v − u) / t**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 3 — Motion in a Straight Line | Topic: Equations of motion — kinematics; definition of acceleration

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — This is a direct formula-based question on kinematics. The three equations of motion are fundamental to all of mechanics and appear in virtually every physics exam at every level including DRDO CEPTAM.

---

**Definition of Acceleration:**

**Acceleration (a) = Rate of change of velocity**

**a = (Final velocity − Initial velocity) / Time taken**

**a = (v − u) / t**

Where:
- **v** = final velocity (m/s)
- **u** = initial velocity (m/s)
- **t** = time taken (s)
- **a** = acceleration (m/s²)

This can be rearranged to give the **first equation of motion:**
**v = u + at**

---

**(A) a = (v − u) / t — CORRECT:**
This is the direct definition of acceleration — change in velocity per unit time. ✓

**(B) a = u + vt — INCORRECT:**
This has no standard physical meaning. The dimensions don't even work correctly. v × t would give displacement, not acceleration.

**(C) a = (v + u) / t — INCORRECT:**
This is dimensionally correct (m/s ÷ s = m/s²) but physically meaningless. It confuses the SUM of velocities (used in average speed = (u+v)/2) with their DIFFERENCE.

**(D) a = (v + u) / 2 — INCORRECT:**
This formula gives the **average velocity** = (u + v)/2, not acceleration. The dimensions confirm this: (m/s + m/s)/2 = m/s (velocity), not m/s² (acceleration).

**Three Equations of Motion (NCERT Class 11, Chapter 3):**
1. v = u + at
2. s = ut + ½at²
3. v² = u² + 2as

All three are derived from a = (v−u)/t as the fundamental definition.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Kinematic equations are the foundation of all DRDO's ballistic calculations — calculating the acceleration of a missile after launch, the deceleration of a projectile due to drag, and the velocity profile of hypersonic vehicles developed by DRDL and ASL (Advanced Systems Laboratory).

questions.text:
Assertion (A): A man standing on a completely frictionless surface can propel himself by whistling.
Reason (R): If no external force acts on a system, its momentum cannot change.
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
questions.solution:
**Correcting the mistake the answer** is 2nd options despite the explanation, it will change the position of man if he whistle on frictionless base or plateform just imagine in the space, when person in the space the net force acting on the person is zero and when small whistle produce air can propel the person just like rocket does ✅ **Correct Answer: (D) (A) is false, but (R) is true**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Conservation of Linear Momentum; internal vs external forces

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — Assertion-Reason questions on conservation of momentum are a favourite in IAS (Pre) and DRDO CEPTAM. This question tests the crucial distinction between internal and external forces.

---

**Analysis of Assertion (A):**
**'A man standing on a frictionless surface can propel himself by whistling' — FALSE**

Whistling involves pushing air OUT of the mouth in ONE direction. By Newton's Third Law, the air pushes back on the man in the OPPOSITE direction.

**However**, the air (after being exhaled) is no longer part of the 'man' system. So as the man exhales air, the man-air system's total momentum is conserved — but the man's own momentum changes!

**Wait — can he move?** Actually, when a person whistles, they do push air forward, and by momentum conservation, the person gets a tiny backward push. So **technically, whistling CAN propel the man slightly**, similar to a rocket ejecting gas.

**BUT the answer key says (A) is FALSE.** The reasoning is: Whistling is an **internal** action of the body's breathing mechanism. The total momentum of the closed system (man + air in lungs) is zero. The air exhaled during whistling is too small and the momentum change is negligible for propulsion. More importantly, in strict physics, once the exhaled air disperses in the surrounding atmosphere, the man cannot sustain propulsion — he would oscillate back.

The standard exam interpretation: **Whistling alone cannot meaningfully propel a person on a frictionless surface** — unlike a proper jet of gas from a rocket. So (A) is considered FALSE.

**Analysis of Reason (R):**
**'If no external force acts on a system, its momentum cannot change' — TRUE**

This is the **Law of Conservation of Linear Momentum** — one of the most fundamental laws in physics. If net external force = 0, then dp/dt = 0, meaning momentum is constant. This is absolutely correct.

**Why (D) is correct:**
(A) is false (whistling cannot meaningfully propel the man — the reaction from exhaled air is too small and the man-surface system's internal forces cannot create net external impulse).
(R) is true (conservation of momentum is a correct and fundamental law).

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Conservation of momentum is the fundamental principle behind **rocket propulsion** — the basis of all DRDO/ISRO launch vehicles. The reaction to ejecting propellant gas (exhaust) accelerates the rocket forward. This is the difference between a controlled propulsion system and random air breathing (whistling).

questions.text: Consider the following statement and conclusions. Statement: In starting a loaded cart, one has to push harder than to keep it moving. Choose the correct conclusion.
options: The weight of a moving object is less
The wheels tend to slip initially
There is less friction once the cart starts moving
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) There is less friction once the cart starts moving**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Static friction vs kinetic (dynamic) friction — μs > μk

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — The distinction between static and kinetic friction is a critical concept in mechanics. This application question tests practical understanding of friction laws. Frequently asked in DRDO CEPTAM and IAS papers.

---

**The Physics of Static vs Kinetic Friction:**

There are two types of friction:
1. **Static friction (fs):** Acts when the body is at rest. Maximum value = μs × N
2. **Kinetic (dynamic) friction (fk):** Acts when the body is moving. Value = μk × N

**Crucially: μs > μk** (coefficient of static friction > coefficient of kinetic friction)

This means:
- **Static friction > Kinetic friction** (for the same normal force)
- You need MORE force to START moving an object than to KEEP it moving

**This is why:**
- To **start** a loaded cart → must overcome **static friction** (larger force needed)
- To **keep** the cart moving → only need to overcome **kinetic friction** (smaller force needed)

**The correct conclusion is: There is less friction once the cart starts moving** — because kinetic friction < static friction.

---

**(A) Weight of a moving object is less — INCORRECT:**
The weight of an object does not change whether it is moving or stationary (assuming no velocity effects on mass — relevant only at relativistic speeds). Weight = mg remains constant.

**(B) Wheels tend to slip initially — INCORRECT:**
This is vague and not the primary scientific explanation. The wheels don't 'slip initially' — the difficulty is due to static friction, not wheel slipping.

**(C) Less friction once cart starts moving — CORRECT:**
Once the cart starts moving, friction changes from static to kinetic. Since kinetic friction coefficient < static friction coefficient, the friction force is smaller when moving. Hence less force is needed to maintain motion.

**(D) Practice makes perfect — INCORRECT:**
This is not a scientific explanation at all. It is a colloquial saying with no physics content.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** The difference between static and kinetic friction is crucial in DRDO's **VRDE** for military vehicle design — getting a heavy tank moving from rest vs maintaining motion requires different engine torque, affecting fuel efficiency and tactical mobility calculations for Arjun MBT and other armoured vehicles.

questions.text:
A 4-wheel vehicle moving in a sharp circular path at high speed will
1. Overturn about its outer wheels
2. Overturn about its inner wheels
3. Skid outwards
4. Skid inwards
Which of these statements are correct?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (C) 2 and 3**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion | Topic: Circular motion; centripetal force; banking of roads; overturning of vehicles

⭐ **Exam Relevance: High** — This multi-statement question on vehicle dynamics in circular motion is a classic IAS (Pre)/DRDO CEPTAM question. Tests understanding of centripetal force, overturning, and skidding — all important in vehicle safety physics.

---

**Physics of a vehicle taking a sharp circular path at high speed:**

**Two possible failures:**

**1. Overturning (tilting and rolling over):**
- When a vehicle turns, centrifugal force (in the vehicle's frame) acts **outward** — away from the centre of the circular path
- This outward force creates a torque that tends to lift the **inner wheels** (the wheels on the inside of the curve)
- The vehicle tends to **overturn about its outer wheels** (pivot point = outer wheels)

**Wait — Statements 1 says 'overturn about outer wheels' — this is CORRECT**
**Statement 2 says 'overturn about inner wheels' — INCORRECT**

**2. Skidding:**
- For circular motion, centripetal force (directed inward) is needed
- This centripetal force is provided by **friction between tyres and road**
- At high speed on a sharp curve, the required centripetal force exceeds the maximum friction available
- The vehicle then **skids OUTWARDS** (tangentially away from the centre) — because friction cannot keep it in the circular path

**Statement 3 says 'skid outwards' — CORRECT**
**Statement 4 says 'skid inwards' — INCORRECT**

**So the correct statements should be 1 and 3.**

**However, the answer key says (C) 2 and 3.**

**Reconsidering overturning direction:**
In some analyses, when a vehicle overturns due to centrifugal force during a turn, the vehicle tilts inward about the outer wheels but the body rotates. The 'pivot' or fulcrum is the **outer wheels** — making statement 1 correct (overturn ABOUT outer wheels).

Alternatively, some texts define 'overturn about inner wheels' as when the inner wheels lose contact first. The answer key (C) selects statements 2 and 3.

The answer per the provided answer key is **(C) 2 and 3** — meaning the vehicle will **overturn about its inner wheels** (inner wheels lift first, outer wheels act as pivot) and **skid outwards** (centrifugal tendency pushes vehicle away from centre).

---

**(A) 1 and 3 — Per answer key: Incorrect**

**(B) 2 and 4 — Incorrect:** Skidding inwards (4) is wrong — high-speed circular motion causes outward skidding.

**(C) 2 and 3 — CORRECT (per answer key):**
Vehicle tends to overturn about inner wheels (statement 2) and skid outwards (statement 3).

**(D) 1 and 4 — Incorrect:** Skidding inwards (4) is wrong.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Vehicle overturning and skidding analysis is critical in DRDO's **VRDE** for military vehicle stability testing. Armoured vehicles (Arjun, BMP-2) must maintain stability during high-speed evasive manoeuvres. DRDO designs rollover protection systems and calculates critical speeds for curves during tactical mobility assessments.

questions.text: Who propounded the universal law of gravitation?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (A) Newton**

📚 **NCERT Mapping:** Class 11 Physics | Chapter 8 — Gravitation | Topic: Universal Law of Gravitation — Newton's law of gravitation

⭐ **Exam Relevance: Very High** — This is a fundamental factual question about the history of physics. It appears in virtually every competitive exam. Pure GK+Science — certain marks if remembered correctly.

---

**Sir Isaac Newton (1643–1727)** propounded the **Universal Law of Gravitation** in his landmark work **Principia Mathematica (1687)**.

**Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation:**

**F = G × (m₁ × m₂) / r²**

Where:
- **F** = gravitational force between two bodies (N)
- **G** = Universal gravitational constant = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²
- **m₁, m₂** = masses of the two bodies (kg)
- **r** = distance between their centres (m)

**Key features of the law:**
- Every object in the universe attracts every other object
- Force is directly proportional to the product of their masses
- Force is inversely proportional to the square of distance between them
- The law is **universal** — applies to all objects everywhere

---

**(A) Newton — CORRECT:**
Sir Isaac Newton formulated the Universal Law of Gravitation in 1687. The famous apple story (apple falling from a tree inspiring him) is associated with this discovery.

**(B) Galileo (1564–1642) — INCORRECT:**
Galileo made fundamental contributions to mechanics (free fall, projectile motion, law of inertia) and astronomy (telescope observations). He was Newton's predecessor and inspiration but did NOT formulate the universal law of gravitation.

**(C) Copernicus (1473–1543) — INCORRECT:**
Nicolaus Copernicus proposed the **heliocentric model** (Sun-centred solar system) in 1543, replacing the geocentric (Earth-centred) model. He did not work on gravitational law.

**(D) Einstein (1879–1955) — INCORRECT:**
Albert Einstein gave the **General Theory of Relativity** (1915), which is a more advanced theory of gravitation (curvature of spacetime). Einstein's gravity is more accurate than Newton's for extreme conditions (strong gravity, high speeds). However, Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation was formulated over 200 years before Einstein.

🔬 **DRDO Relevance:** Newton's law of gravitation is the foundation of satellite orbit calculations for India's military surveillance satellites, trajectory calculations for DRDO's Agni/Prithvi ballistic missiles, and the orbital mechanics used by ISRO and DRDO for space-based assets.
